# 2. Defining a FAIR Brain Network Model {background-color="#6880af"}

## FAIR Guiding Principles

![[@TuringCommunity2024]](img/fair/reproducible-definitions.svg){fig-align="center" height="600px"}

## FAIR Principles

![[@TuringCommunity2024]](img/fair/fair-principles.svg){fig-align="center" height="600px"}

## A Simulation Experiment in Computational Neuroscience

![](img/tvbo/tvbo-network-insets.png)

## A Simulation Experiment in Computational Neuroscience

```{=html}
<style>
.se-dia {
  font-family: system-ui, -apple-system, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif;
  background: linear-gradient(145deg, #f4f8f8, #e4edef);
  border: 2px solid #9ab5b8; border-radius: 18px;
  padding: 20px 22px 18px; width: 100%; box-sizing:border-box;
  margin: 0 auto; box-shadow: 0 6px 32px rgba(43,122,120,.1);
}
.se-dia .hdr { text-align:center; font-size:1.15em; font-weight:700; color:#2b6b6b; letter-spacing:.03em; margin-bottom:12px; }
.se-dia .top { display:grid; grid-template-columns:3fr 2fr; grid-template-rows:auto auto; gap:10px; margin-bottom:12px; }
.se-dia .top .net { grid-row:1/3; }
.se-dia .bot { display:flex; gap:10px; }
.se-dia .g { border:1.5px dashed; border-radius:12px; padding:10px 12px 12px; min-width:0; display:flex; flex-direction:column; }
.se-dia .gl { font-size:.54em; font-weight:700; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.09em; margin-bottom:6px; padding-left:2px; }
.se-dia .pp { display:flex; flex-wrap:wrap; gap:5px 6px; align-items:center; }
.se-dia .p  { display:inline-flex; align-items:center; padding:4px 10px; border-radius:16px; font-size:.52em; font-weight:500; white-space:nowrap; border:1.2px solid; line-height:1.3; }
.se-dia .sub { border-radius:9px; padding:7px 10px 8px; margin-top:7px; }
.se-dia .sub .gl { font-size:.48em; margin-bottom:3px; }
.se-dia .f1  { flex:1; }
.se-dia .f08 { flex:.8; }
.se-dia .arr { font-size:.48em; color:#6a9a9a; margin:0 2px; line-height:1; font-weight:300; }
.se-dia .p.opt { border-style:dashed; opacity:.7; }
/* Network: blue */
.g.c-net        { border-color:#8bbde0; background:rgba(200,225,240,.25); }
.g.c-net > .gl  { color:#2e6e9c; }
.p.c-net        { background:#9dd0f0; border-color:#5ea8d4; color:#1a4a6e; }
.g.c-net .sub        { border:1.2px solid #a8d0e8; background:rgba(210,230,245,.35); }
.g.c-net .sub .gl    { color:#4a8ab8; }
.p.c-dyn        { background:#bee0f4; border-color:#7ec0e0; color:#1e5a80; }
.p.c-cpl        { background:#d0e8f6; border-color:#98cce6; color:#2a6890; }
/* Integration: teal */
.g.c-int        { border-color:#7abab8; background:rgba(180,220,218,.25); }
.g.c-int > .gl  { color:#2b6b6b; }
.p.c-int        { background:#8ad0cc; border-color:#52a8a4; color:#1a4e4e; }
/* Event: amber */
.g.c-evt        { border-color:#d4c4a0; background:rgba(230,218,195,.25); }
.g.c-evt > .gl  { color:#8a6a30; }
.p.c-evt        { background:#e8d4b0; border-color:#c8a870; color:#5a4420; }
.g.c-evt .sub        { border:1.2px solid #d8c8a8; background:rgba(235,225,205,.35); }
.g.c-evt .sub .gl    { color:#9a7a40; }
.p.c-stm        { background:#f0e0c4; border-color:#d4b888; color:#6a5030; }
/* Observation: cyan-teal */
.g.c-obs        { border-color:#88c4c4; background:rgba(190,225,225,.25); }
.g.c-obs > .gl  { color:#2e7070; }
.p.c-obs        { background:#98d4d0; border-color:#5eb0ac; color:#1a5050; }
/* Exploration: green */
.g.c-exp        { border-color:#98c8a4; background:rgba(200,230,210,.25); }
.g.c-exp > .gl  { color:#3a7048; }
.p.c-exp        { background:#a8d8b4; border-color:#70b480; color:#1e4a2a; }
/* Analysis: slate blue */
.g.c-ana        { border-color:#98a8c4; background:rgba(200,210,228,.25); }
.g.c-ana > .gl  { color:#3a5070; }
.p.c-ana        { background:#b0c0d8; border-color:#7898b8; color:#1e3450; }
/* Environment: warm gray */
.g.c-env        { border-color:#b0b8b8; background:rgba(215,220,220,.25); }
.g.c-env > .gl  { color:#505858; }
.p.c-env        { background:#c4cccc; border-color:#94a0a0; color:#2a3434; }
</style>
<div class="se-dia">
  <div class="hdr">SimulationExperiment</div>
  <div class="top">
    <div class="g c-net net">
      <div class="gl">Network</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-net opt">Parcellation</span>
        <span class="arr">⟶</span>
        <span class="p c-net">Node</span>
        <span class="arr">⟶</span>
        <span class="p c-net">Edge</span>
      </div>
      <div class="sub">
        <div class="gl">Dynamics</div>
        <div class="pp">
          <span class="p c-dyn">StateVariable</span>
          <span class="p c-dyn">Parameter</span>
          <span class="p c-dyn">Equation</span>
          <span class="p c-dyn">DerivedVariable</span>
          <span class="p c-dyn">DerivedParameter</span>
          <span class="p c-dyn">Function</span>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="sub">
        <div class="gl">Coupling</div>
        <div class="pp">
          <span class="p c-cpl">Parameter</span>
          <span class="p c-cpl">Equation</span>
          <span class="p c-cpl">IncomingState</span>
          <span class="p c-cpl">LocalState</span>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="g c-int">
      <div class="gl">Integration</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-int">Method</span>
        <span class="p c-int">Noise</span>
        <span class="p c-int">InitialCondition</span>
        <span class="p c-int">TimeSeries</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="g c-evt">
      <div class="gl">Event</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-evt">Equation</span>
        <span class="p c-evt">Target</span>
      </div>
      <div class="sub">
        <div class="gl">Stimulus</div>
        <div class="pp">
          <span class="p c-stm">Waveform</span>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="bot">
    <div class="g c-obs f1">
      <div class="gl">Observation</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-obs">Source</span>
        <span class="p c-obs">Pipeline</span>
        <span class="arr">⟶</span>
        <span class="p c-obs">DataSource</span>
        <span class="p c-obs">DerivedObs.</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="g c-exp f08">
      <div class="gl">Exploration</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-exp">Exploration</span>
        <span class="p c-exp">Algorithm</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="g c-ana f08">
      <div class="gl">Analysis</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-ana">Optimization</span>
        <span class="p c-ana">Continuation</span>
      </div>
    </div>
    <div class="g c-env f1">
      <div class="gl">Environment</div>
      <div class="pp">
        <span class="p c-env">SoftwareEnv.</span>
        <span class="p c-env">Execution</span>
        <span class="p c-env">Reference</span>
        <span class="p c-env">Author</span>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

## The Virtual Brain Ontology (TVB-O) {#tvbo-intro}

A brain network model usually lives in a methods section — prose you can *read* but rarely **re-run**. TVB-O makes the **model itself a FAIR, machine-readable artifact**:

::: {.incremental}
- <i class="fa-solid fa-diagram-project"></i> **Common vocabulary** — ontology-backed terms with persistent identifiers (`w3id.org/tvbo`); no more ambiguous symbols across papers
- <i class="fa-solid fa-file-code"></i> **Machine-readable metadata** — a compact YAML schema captures everything needed to reproduce a simulation
- <i class="fa-solid fa-database"></i> **Curated database** — published models, connectomes and study configurations, ready to load and run
- <i class="fa-solid fa-gears"></i> **Code generation** — one specification → executable code for TVB, JAX, Julia, …
:::

![[@Martin2025]](img/figures/tvbo/tvbo_logo.png){fig-align="center" height=120px}

<style>
#tvbo-intro ul { list-style:none; margin-left:0; }
#tvbo-intro li { margin:.4em 0; }
#tvbo-intro li i.fa-solid { color:#2b7a78; width:1.5em; text-align:center; margin-right:.5em; }
</style>

::: {.notes}
The single slide that introduces the ontology the whole course builds on. The problem: a model
published in a paper is prose plus symbols — human-readable but not machine-actionable, so reproducing
it is slow and error-prone. TVB-O makes the specification *itself* the shareable object, and four
things follow: a common ontology-backed vocabulary (terms resolve to persistent identifiers), a
compact machine-readable YAML schema, a curated database of ready-to-run models and connectomes, and
code generation to multiple backends (TVB, JAX, Julia). The next slides show what that specification
looks like in practice.
:::

## Model Specification with TVB-O

:::: {.columns}


::: {.column width=35%}

**Metadata Schema**
```yaml
id: 21
model: Dynamics
dynamics:
  name: Dynamics
  parameters:
    a:
      name: a
      value: 1.0
  state_variables:
    x:
      name: x
      equation:
        rhs: a*x
      initial_value: 1.0
integration:
  method: Heun
  time_scale: ms
  duration: 4.0
  step_size: 0.01
explorations:
  name: a_sweep
  space:
  - parameter: a
    explored_values:
    - 1.0
    - 0.0
    - -1.0
```
:::

::: {.column width=65%}

**Python API**
```python
from tvbo import DynamicalSystem, SimulationExperiment
from tvbo.datamodel.schema import Exploration, ExplorationAxis

bsplot.style.use("tvbo")

g = DynamicalSystem(
    state_variables={"x": {
                          "equation": {"rhs": "a*x"},
                          "initial_value": 1.0}},
    parameters={"a": {"value": 1.0}},
)

exp = SimulationExperiment(dynamics=g)

exp.explorations = Exploration(
    name="a_sweep",
    space=ExplorationAxis(
      parameter="a",
      explored_values=[1.0, 0, -1.0]
      ),
)

exp.integration.duration = 4.0
exp.integration.step_size = 0.01

res = exp.run("tvboptim")
```
:::


::::

## Specifying an experiment with TVB-O

![](img/figures/tvbo/fig-overview.png){height=680px fig-align="center"}

## Build an experiment in the browser

No code — assemble a model, connectome and stimulus, then run it. The platform writes the reproducible specification for you.

![](img/videos/tvbo-experiment-builder-workflow.mp4){width="820px" height="540px" fig-align="center" autoplay="" loop="" muted="" controls=""}

::: {.notes}
This is the TVB-O experiment builder in the browser: choose a neural-mass model, attach a
connectome, add a stimulus, and launch — point and click. Under the hood it produces exactly the
machine-readable specification we just saw, so even a click-through experiment stays fully
reproducible and shareable.
:::

## ...or run it in Python

The very same specification, driven from a notebook — for scripting, parameter sweeps and pipelines.

![](img/videos/tvbo-run-in-python.mp4){width="820px" height="540px" fig-align="center" autoplay="" loop="" muted="" controls=""}

::: {.notes}
The builder and the Python API are two front-ends to one specification. Prototype visually, then
move to code for parameter sweeps, HPC runs or embedding in a pipeline — without re-specifying
anything. This is the bridge from the no-code demo to the hands-on part of the course.
:::

## ...or explore the ontology and simulate in JupyterLab

`tvb-ext-ontology` turns the model ontology into an interactive graph: search for a neural-mass model, inspect its definition and ontology relationships, add it to a workspace, pick a connectome — then **Run Simulation**, without ever leaving JupyterLab.

![](img/videos/tvbo-ext-ontology-simulation.mp4){width="720px" height="548px" fig-align="center" autoplay="" loop="" muted="" controls=""}

::: {.notes}
A third front-end onto the same specification — this time inside JupyterLab. The `tvb-ext-ontology`
extension renders the TVB-O ontology as a force-directed graph you can search and explore. Click a
neural-mass model to read its definition and see its parents and children in the ontology, then add
it to the workspace. Choose a parcellation and tractogram for the connectome and press Run
Simulation: the server builds the experiment straight from the ontology and runs it, writing
BIDS-formatted results that appear right in the file browser, ready to open in a notebook. Browse,
assemble, simulate — all from the ontology graph itself.
:::

## Try TVB-O yourself {.smaller}

Three front-ends, one specification — scan to open.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Platform** · [tvbo.charite.de](https://tvbo.charite.de/)

![](img/qr/preview-tvbo-platform.png){height="240px" fig-align="center" style="border:1px solid #ddd"}

![](img/qr/qr-tvbo-platform.png){height="150px" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Python package** · `pip install tvbo`

![](img/qr/preview-tvbo-pypi.png){height="240px" fig-align="center" style="border:1px solid #ddd"}

![](img/qr/qr-tvbo-pypi.png){height="150px" fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**JupyterLab extension** · `pip install tvb-ext-ontology`

![](img/qr/preview-tvb-ext-ontology-pypi.png){height="240px" fig-align="center" style="border:1px solid #ddd"}

![](img/qr/qr-tvb-ext-ontology-pypi.png){height="150px" fig-align="center"}
:::

::::

::: {.notes}
Everything shown is public and installable today. The platform at tvbo.charite.de runs the browser
builder and the ontology knowledge graph; `pip install tvbo` gives you the Python package and the
machine-readable specification; `pip install tvb-ext-ontology` adds the JupyterLab extension you
just saw. Same specification underneath all three — scan whichever way of working suits you.
:::

## In short {#recap-framework background-iframe="brain-recap.html" background-color="#0a1622"}

::: {.recap-box}
A brain network model is **local dynamics on a structural connectome**, observed through a **forward model**.

::: {.incremental}
- <i class="fa-solid fa-wave-square"></i> **Regions** carry neural-mass **dynamics** — the local node model
- <i class="fa-solid fa-diagram-project"></i> Wired by the **connectome** — weights and conduction **delays**
- <i class="fa-solid fa-chart-line"></i> A **forward model** maps activity to **BOLD, EEG, MEG**
:::

::: {.recap-tvbo}
**TVB-O** isn't a piece of software — it's a whole **framework** that harmonizes *how* such models are specified, so any of them stays shareable and re-runnable.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Recap of the framework before we turn to applications: a brain network model couples local regional
dynamics over the structural connectome — with connection weights and conduction delays — and a forward
model turns the simulated activity into measurable signals like BOLD, EEG or MEG. That whole construct is
what's rotating here. TVB-O is not a tool you run; it's a framework for harmonizing how those models are
specified, so any of them can be shared and re-run.
:::

<style>
#recap-framework h2 { color: #eef4ff; }
#recap-framework .recap-box {
  width: 46%;
  color: #e6eef9;
  font-size: .92em;
  line-height: 1.42;
}
#recap-framework .recap-box strong { color: #ffffff; }
#recap-framework .recap-box ul { list-style: none; margin-left: 0; padding-left: 0; margin-top: .45em; }
#recap-framework .recap-box li { margin: .34em 0; }
#recap-framework .recap-box li i.fa-solid { color: #5fd0c5; width: 1.5em; text-align: center; margin-right: .35em; }
#recap-framework .recap-tvbo {
  margin-top: .7em;
  padding-top: .5em;
  border-top: 1px solid rgba(95,208,197,.35);
  font-size: .8em;
  color: #b9c6d6;
}
#recap-framework .recap-tvbo strong { color: #5fd0c5; }
</style>

## Applications in basic research

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

![](img/figures/applications/Schirner2023Title.png)

![[@Schirner2023]](img/videos/Schirner2023videosnippet.mp4){width="100%" autoplay="" loop="" muted=""}

:::

::: {.column}

![](img/figures/applications/Koller2024Title.png)

![[@Koller2024]](img/videos/travelling_waves.mp4){width="100%" autoplay="" loop="" muted=""}

:::

::::
